result.markdown:
---
title: "Recruitment conditioning"
execute:
  dir: project
---



## Recruitment dynamics and stock-recruitment relationship

Recruitment dynamics in the Operating Model (OM) were conditioned using historical spawning stock biomass (SSB) and recruitment estimates derived from the final Stock Synthesis (SS3) assessment for anchovy in Division 9a South (ane.27.9aS). 

Recruitment was assumed to occur in the third quarter (Q3), while spawning stock biomass was evaluated in the second quarter (Q2), consistent with the biological timing implemented in the assessment model.

A Beverton-Holt stock-recruitment (SR) relationship was fitted to the historical SSB and recruitment series using the FLSR framework. The fitted OM SR relationship was subsequently compared with the Beverton-Holt parameterization estimated within SS3 to verify consistency between the assessment model and the operating model. Predicted recruitment values from both formulations were compared across the observed range of SSB, and differences in expected recruitment and residuals patterns were quantified.

To facilitate interpretation of recruitment uncertainty, recruitment envelopes corresponding to the estimated recruitment variability were calculated around the SS3 stock-recruitment relationship.  Median recruitment predictions were combined with lognormal recruitment deviations using the estimated recruitment standard deviation ($\sigma_R$) from the assessment model.

### Recruitment residual diagnostics

Historical recruitment residuals were calculated as:


$\varepsilon_t = \log(R_{obs,t})-\log(R_{pred,t})$

where ($R_{obst,t}$) represents observed recruitment and ($R_{pred,t}$) the recruitment predicted by the fitted Beverton-Holt relationship.

The distributional properties of recruitment residuals were evaluated to determine whether the assumptions commonly adopted in fisheries simulated models were supported by the historical data.

Diagnostics included:

- Time-series plots of recruitment residuals
- Histograms and kernel density estimates
- Quantile-quantile (QQ) plots
- Summary statistics including mean, standard deviation, skewness and kurtosis
- Comparison of observed residual quantiles with those expected under a lognormal recruitment process.

Normality of residuals was evaluated using the Shapiro-Wilk test. The objective was not to formally validate a strict normal distribution, but rather to identify substantial departures from the lognormal assumption commonly used in stock assessment and management strategy evaluation frameworks.

## Results

### Historical recruitment dynamics

Historical recruitment exhibited substantial interannual variability throughout the period 1989–2024, ranging approximately from 3.5 to 12 million recruits. No persistent increasing or decreasing trend was evident, and no prolonged periods of consistently low recruitment were observed.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![xxx](../../outputs/recruitment_conditioning/figures/01_historical_recruitment_timeseries.png){fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::
:::



### Stock-recruitment relationship

The Beverton–Holt relationship fitted within the Operating Model closely reproduced the stock–recruitment relationship estimated by the SS3 assessment. Differences between both curves were small across the observed range of spawning biomass, indicating that the OM adequately captures the productivity assumptions of the assessment model.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![xxx](../../outputs/recruitment_conditioning/figures/02_SR_curve_OM_vs_SS3_sigmaR.png){fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::
:::




::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Stock-recruitment parameters used to compare the OM and SS3 Beverton-Holt relationships.

|parameter          |       value|source       |
|:------------------|-----------:|:------------|
|a_OM               | 8926007.024|FLSR OM fit  |
|b_OM               |    1898.343|FLSR OM fit  |
|R0_SS3             | 8291985.468|SS3          |
|h_SS3              |       0.800|SS3          |
|SSB0_SS3           |   17217.700|SS3          |
|sigmaR_SS3_or_used |       0.330|SS3          |
|sigmaR_OM_resid    |       0.280|OM residuals |


:::
:::


### Recruitment residuals diagnostics

Recruitment residuals were approximately symmetric and centred around zero. The histogram and QQ plot indicated a reasonable agreement with the assumed lognormal error structure, although some deviations were observed in the tails of the distribution, particularly for a small number of low-recruitment years.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![xxx](../../outputs/recruitment_conditioning/figures/04_OM_residual_distribution_vs_sigmaR.png){fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![xxx](../../outputs/recruitment_conditioning/figures/05_OM_residual_QQplot.png){fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::
:::




::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Recruitment residual diagnostic tests.

|test                   | statistic| p_value|
|:----------------------|---------:|-------:|
|Ljung-Box lag 1        |     0.545|   0.460|
|Ljung-Box lag 3        |     3.454|   0.327|
|Ljung-Box lag 5        |     7.656|   0.176|
|Shapiro-Wilk normality |     0.946|   0.080|


:::
:::

Table: Candidate recruitment scenarios for the MSE Operating Model.

|base                    |Beverton-Holt |         1.00|         0.28|    0.00|Reference OM based on fitted BH and observed recruitment residuals.                 |

|high_sigmaR_150         |Beverton-Holt |         1.00|         0.42|    0.00|Strong increase in recruitment variability relative to ane9aS residuals.            |
|low_productivity_075    |Beverton-Holt |         0.75|         0.28|    0.00|Moderate low-productivity regime.                                                   |
|low_productivity_050    |Beverton-Holt |         0.50|         0.28|    0.00|Severe low-productivity regime.                                                     |
|AR1_recruitment_Thorson |Beverton-Holt |         1.00|         0.28|    0.43|Exploratory scenario using Thorson et al. 2014 mean autocorrelation.                |
